key_insight: In 316H stainless steel, nanoscratch loading drives a pressure- and shear-controlled sequence of martensitic transformations, typically γ→ε→α′, with local constraint determining which pathway dominates.
engineering_relevance: These results explain how contact mechanics and microstructure interact to control tribolayer formation and galling resistance in stainless steels and related hardfacing alloys.
method: In-situ synchrotron X-ray nanodiffractometry during nanoscratch testing
Spatial mapping of evolving strain and phase fields beneath a single asperity
Finite element modelling to interpret pressure, shear, and martensite-pathway selection
evidence_signal: The abstract reports spatially resolved phase/strain maps and model interpretation that distinguish where γ→ε, ε→α′, or direct γ→α′ transformations occur under the scratch contact.
watch_item: The mechanism is inferred from a single-asperity nanoscratch geometry, so extension to real multi-asperity service contacts may require further validation.
paper_title: Spatially resolved in-situ characterisation of competing martensitic transformation pathways during nanoscratch in 316H Stainless Steel
